name: syntax-parse
summary: Ryan Culpepper and Matthias Felleisen's Racket parser for macro inputs (ICFP 2010 "Fortifying Macros"; JFP 2012). Specification-driven error attribution above the reader/syntax-object level.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Ryan Culpepper and Matthias Felleisen's syntax-parse (ICFP 2010 "Fortifying Macros"; JFP 22(4–5) 2012 extended version) is Racket's parser for macro inputs. Racket's reader turns text into syntax objects; syntax-parse is the layer above that turns a macro's received syntax object into structured data, with error messages that explain misuse.

In Racket this is not a niche tool: every non-trivial `define-syntax` in the standard library is written against syntax-parse, and the syntax classes themselves are the standard way to document "what a valid macro call looks like."

sections.title: Syntax classes
sections.content: A macro author writes a pattern — for example `(my-for ([x:id seq] ...) body ...+)` — using syntax classes like `id`, `expr`, or user-defined classes with their own pattern structure, optional annotations, side conditions, and attributes. Syntax classes give macro authors a way to define reusable input categories with constraints and associated structure, supporting modular macro APIs and more precise error reporting than ad hoc syntax destructuring.

sections.title: Deterministic error attribution
sections.content:
When a macro is called with syntactically ill-formed arguments, syntax-parse does not emit an opaque "bad syntax". It points at the specific sub-form that failed to match and explains which alternative was expected.

The 2012 paper formalises the system as a pattern language with deterministic error attribution: given several failed alternatives, syntax-parse picks the one that got furthest into the input and reports that, mirroring Parsec/Megaparsec error-quality heuristics but at the syntax-object level.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content: syntax-parse delivers specification-driven macro error messages comparable to a parser generator's diagnostics — at the cost of a second parsing layer above the reader and of being Racket-specific in a way that readtables, Pratt, and combinators are not. The expressiveness depends on Racket's syntax-object model with hygienic source location; reusing the design outside Racket would require those primitives.
